He's hypersensitive to language. He's doing this on the fly, but he's trained and conditioned himself to be hypersensitive to language. Copywriters, good ones, are hypersensitive to language. So if for a 16-page sales ladder, which will take roughly the average person 30, 48 minutes to read, you would agonize for an hour over a choice of a word or a sentence. Why wouldn't you put equal angst into getting it right in a 48-minute face-to-face sales presentation? In some respects, the stakes are higher because direct mail, if you get 2% conversion, you're a hero. If you're selling in the Cadillac dealership and you only convert 2% of the people you meet with, you aren't going to be in the Cadillac dealership very long, right? So why wouldn't you? Some pros, real pros, the correct thing to do is to write it word for word until you have it right. Even if you want to back out of that to a structural outline that is all you use to refresh yourself, fine. But just to do the outline, it's better than nothing, but it is not real professional selling. It's just not. Yeah, and so I do a lot of stage presentations as do you, Dan. And if I'm going to script anything word for word, it's going to absolutely be my opening that the first things that are coming out of my mouth. The thing I'm going to script the least is the actual teaching portion of it. But then my close, oh baby, that thing is scripted word for word. Also again, I think people think that if it's not scripted, I have more freedom. I think it's the opposite. I think it's the opposite. Well, it depends on how you define freedom. Well, I mean being able to really make the sale and focus on my prospect. Exactly. It gives you greater freedom to focus on the other person or persons, right. And feedback, body or spoken, all of that because you have a solid foundation underneath you that you've already learned, preferably memorized, rehearsed, or familiar with. So you have no part of you as to be thinking about what I'm going to say, right. It does of course restrain you to a path. So if you want the freedom to just roam, if you define freedom that way, right. So again, what I often do when I'm brought into my copy for a company that has salespeople, humans at the end of the chain is I will record them and get that transcribed. And it's usually horrifying, right. Absolutely. It is usually horrifying for me because I know I am going to have some accountability for a result that I am delivering prospects into the hands of these roaming free creatures. It's horrifying for the client because I immediately go and say, right. And very often the same person, three presentations in a row you wouldn't know, not only when you know it was the same person, you wouldn't know it was the same product or the same company. Now there has to be of six stories that make point A. There's got to be one that's more effective than the other. There has to be. Why wouldn't we want to use the most effective one every single time, right. So it's a false freedom to be left alone to roam. Yeah, absolutely. And again, one of the bonuses that you got to keep referring to is Nick's script book that he put together. So again, you want to go through this and structure these scripts work, right. So these aren't just things that are tested. So go through and model them for your own scripts. Okay. So in this segment, we've gone over the foundations of sales success and we actually went over the six steps in the No BS sales process. In the next session, we're going to be talking about how you develop and how you create a really powerful, irresistible offer. So that's coming up in part two and you absolutely positively don't want to miss it.
